explanation: Weight-for-height below minus 3 z scores indicates severe wasting and therefore severe acute malnutrition even in the absence of edema. Height-for-age above minus 2 does not show stunting, and recent diarrhea may explain but does not alter the anthropometric category.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A government school implements weekly iron-folic acid supplementation for adolescents. Students are screened for contraindications, tablets are administered under supervision and deworming is coordinated according to programme guidance. A 14-year-old is neither pregnant nor receiving therapeutic iron for diagnosed severe anemia. Which weekly formulation is used under the adolescent programme?
options: A tablet containing 5 mg iron and no folic acid
Daily 200 mg intravenous iron for every student
A tablet containing 60 mg elemental iron and 500 micrograms folic acid
Vitamin A 200,000 IU every week
explanation: Weekly iron-folic acid supplementation for adolescents uses a supervised tablet containing 60 mg elemental iron and 500 micrograms folic acid, alongside nutrition education and periodic deworming. Diagnosed anemia still requires evaluation and treatment rather than assuming prophylaxis alone is sufficient.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: Several students report nausea after taking weekly iron-folic acid on an empty stomach. There is no severe reaction, tablet quality is acceptable and the school considers abandoning the programme. Teachers can supervise ingestion and counsel families. Which response best preserves adherence?
options: Stop supplementation permanently for all students
Double the weekly dose to overcome nausea
Replace iron with antibiotics
Give the weekly tablet after food and provide side-effect counselling
explanation: Taking IFA after food and explaining transient gastrointestinal effects can improve adherence, while supervised administration and referral for persistent symptoms maintain safety. Programme discontinuation sacrifices anemia prevention, and dose doubling or antibiotics do not address routine iron intolerance.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: An urban local body plans a primary-care facility near a large slum cluster. The catchment has roughly 55,000 residents, many mobile workers and poor access to fixed daytime services. Outreach through community workers and convenient outpatient hours are essential. Which NUHM facility best fits this catchment?
options: Urban Primary Health Centre
Rural subcentre
Tertiary superspecialty hospital only
Community health centre for 120,000 rural residents
explanation: Under the NUHM implementation framework, an Urban Primary Health Centre commonly serves about 50,000-60,000 people, preferably near vulnerable settlements and linked with outreach and referral services. Urban planning must account for slums, mobility and convenient timings rather than transplanting rural facility labels mechanically.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A city has a functioning UPHC, but homeless people, migrants and construction workers rarely attend. Clinic records show high coverage among nearby formal housing and low coverage in unlisted settlements. Managers can fund mobile outreach, community mapping and flexible sessions. Which response best reflects NUHM's equity focus?
options: Use only facility attendees as the denominator
Map vulnerable groups and provide targeted outreach linked to the UPHC
Close outreach because a UPHC building exists
Require permanent-address proof before care
explanation: NUHM prioritizes the urban poor and other vulnerable populations through mapping, community processes, outreach and linkage to fixed facilities. Facility-only denominators conceal exclusion, while documentation barriers and absence of outreach worsen inequity despite apparent service availability.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A primary-care team is converted into an Ayushman Arogya Mandir and continues maternal-child and communicable-disease services. The catchment also has untreated hypertension, mental-health needs, oral disease and palliative-care gaps. Staff suggest that comprehensive primary care should still be limited to pregnancy and childhood. Which service model is intended?
options: Only delivery and immunization services
Only inpatient surgery
Twelve expanded comprehensive primary health-care packages
Only insurance authorization without clinical care
explanation: Ayushman Arogya Mandirs are intended to deliver expanded comprehensive primary health care across twelve service packages, including NCDs, mental health, oral, eye and ENT care, elderly and palliative services alongside traditional RMNCH and communicable-disease work. Referral linkage and continuity are integral.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A patient with stable diabetes receives medicines from a primary-care team but has no recall system, complication screening or referral feedback. Each visit is treated as an isolated encounter, and records do not follow the patient between levels. The district wants to apply a core primary-care principle rather than add another one-day camp. Which principle is most deficient?
options: Episodic specialist care only
Maximum centralization of every service
Selective care without follow-up
Continuity and coordination of care
explanation: Comprehensive primary care requires longitudinal continuity, coordinated referral and counter-referral, accessible first contact and a person- and family-centered approach. Repeated isolated camps may increase contact but do not create an accountable care pathway for chronic disease.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A flood-prone district strengthens embankments, enforces safer land use and relocates critical equipment above expected water levels years before the next monsoon. No warning has yet been issued for a specific event, and the aim is to reduce hazard impact permanently. The measures will remain after the current planning cycle. Which disaster-management phase best describes these actions?
options: Mitigation
Preparedness
Response
Recovery
explanation: Mitigation comprises structural and nonstructural measures taken to reduce long-term hazard exposure or vulnerability, such as land-use regulation and resilient infrastructure. Preparedness builds readiness for an event, response is immediate life-saving action and recovery restores and improves affected systems.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A cyclone warning is issued seventy-two hours before predicted landfall. The district activates the incident plan, checks emergency stocks, tests communication chains and rehearses evacuation routes, but the storm has not struck. Long-term coastal zoning was completed earlier. Which phase is now dominant?
options: Mitigation
Preparedness
Response
Reconstruction
explanation: Preparedness consists of planning, training, stockpiling, early-warning action, communication testing and evacuation readiness before impact. Mitigation addresses enduring risk reduction, while response begins with immediate actions during or after impact and reconstruction belongs to recovery.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A mass-casualty incident overwhelms local operating capacity. The first triage officer begins prolonged resuscitation of one casualty and stops classifying others, creating an unsafe bottleneck. Definitive treatment teams are available in a separate zone. Which principle should guide the initial triage officer?
options: Complete definitive treatment before seeing the next casualty
Assign priority by arrival order alone
Rapidly categorize all casualties and repeat triage as conditions change
Use social status to determine color
explanation: Primary disaster triage is rapid, needs-based and resource-aware, aiming to classify many casualties before definitive treatment; lifesaving actions are limited to those allowed by the triage system. Triage is dynamic, so reassessment is essential as physiology and resources change.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: Several agencies arrive after an industrial explosion and issue conflicting evacuation and hospital-distribution orders. Radio channels are duplicated, resource requests bypass logistics and no common situation report exists. Clinical capacity is adequate but coordination failure is worsening delay. Which organizational response is most important?
options: Allow every agency to maintain independent priorities
Eliminate all written situation reports
Send every casualty to the nearest hospital
Establish a unified incident command structure
explanation: An incident command system defines leadership, operations, planning, logistics and finance functions and can create unified command across agencies. It supports common objectives, communication and resource allocation, preventing uncoordinated hospital overload and contradictory field orders.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: In a cohort, coronary-event risk is 12% among smokers and 4% among otherwise comparable nonsmokers over ten years. Follow-up is complete, and confounding is adequately controlled for the teaching calculation. The programme wants the excess absolute risk among exposed people attributable to smoking. What is the attributable risk?
options: 8 percentage points
3 percentage points
33.3 percentage points
200 percentage points
explanation: Attributable risk among the exposed is incidence in exposed minus incidence in unexposed: 12%-4%=8 percentage points. The risk ratio is 3, while the attributable fraction among exposed is (12-4)/12, approximately 66.7%; these answer different public-health questions.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: Two populations have the same smoking-associated risk ratio, but smoking prevalence is 10% in one and 60% in the other. Causal assumptions hold and baseline risks are similar. Policymakers want the proportion of all coronary events in each population that could be prevented if smoking were eliminated. Which measure will differ most because exposure prevalence differs?
options: Risk ratio among smokers
Population attributable fraction
Sensitivity of the case definition
Case fatality among coronary cases
explanation: Population attributable fraction combines the exposure-disease association with exposure prevalence, so it rises when a harmful exposure is more common. The risk ratio can remain identical across populations, while test sensitivity and case fatality describe different processes.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: Coffee consumption appears associated with myocardial infarction, but coffee drinkers in the dataset smoke more often. Smoking is associated with infarction and is not an intermediate consequence of coffee exposure. The crude coffee estimate moves substantially toward the null after smoking adjustment. Which phenomenon is demonstrated?
options: Effect modification by smoking
Random misclassification only
Confounding by smoking
Lead-time bias
explanation: A confounder is associated with the exposure and independently affects the outcome without lying on the causal pathway, distorting the crude association. The change after appropriate adjustment supports confounding; effect modification instead means the true association differs across strata and should be reported.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A vaccine has 90% effectiveness in adults under 60 but 45% effectiveness in adults 60 and older, with narrow confidence intervals and minimal confounding in both strata. A single adjusted estimate hides this consistent biological difference. Stratum-specific risks were prespecified before analysis. Which concept best describes age in this analysis?
options: Confounder that must only be removed
Selection-bias indicator
Intermediate variable caused by vaccination
Effect modifier
explanation: Effect modification is present when the exposure effect truly differs across levels of another variable, here age. It is a finding to describe with stratum-specific estimates and interaction assessment, not merely a nuisance to adjust away as with confounding.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: During an outbreak, attack rate is 2% among fully vaccinated children and 10% among comparable unvaccinated children. Case ascertainment and exposure opportunity are similar, and confounding is negligible for the calculation. The programme estimates vaccine effectiveness from the risk ratio. What is the result?
explanation: Risk ratio is 0.02/0.10=0.20, and vaccine effectiveness is (1-RR) x 100=(1-0.20) x 100=80%. This is a relative reduction; absolute risk reduction is eight percentage points and depends on baseline risk.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A prevention trial reports risk of 6% in controls and 4% with intervention over one year. Follow-up is complete and harms are analyzed separately. Policymakers want the number of people who must receive the intervention to prevent one event during that period. What is the number needed to treat?
explanation: Absolute risk reduction is 6%-4%=2 percentage points, or 0.02, so NNT is 1/0.02=50 over the specified one-year horizon. NNT must be interpreted with baseline risk, follow-up duration, confidence interval, adverse effects and implementation feasibility.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A country detects an unusual cluster with potential for serious international spread. Etiology is uncertain, but the event may meet the decision-instrument criteria for notification under the International Health Regulations. Officials propose waiting for definitive laboratory proof before informing WHO. Which obligation is most appropriate?
options: Wait until every case is laboratory confirmed
Notify only neighboring hospitals
Notify WHO promptly when IHR criteria are met
Close all borders automatically without risk assessment
explanation: The IHR require countries to assess events using the Annex 2 decision instrument and notify WHO through the National IHR Focal Point within the specified time once a potentially notifiable event is assessed, even when details remain incomplete. Notification enables verification and coordinated risk assessment.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A point of entry can detect ill travelers but lacks laboratory referral, risk communication and rapid response capacity. Authorities treat border screening as a substitute for the wider health system. An imported pathogen begins community transmission before symptoms are obvious. Which IHR principle is most relevant?
options: Border temperature checks prevent every importation
Only national laboratories need preparedness
International coordination begins after a pandemic ends
Countries need surveillance and response core capacities beyond points of entry
explanation: IHR preparedness requires national core capacities for surveillance, laboratories, risk assessment, reporting, response and risk communication, with appropriate capacities at designated points of entry. Border measures alone miss presymptomatic or asymptomatic infection and cannot replace resilient domestic systems.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A country expands essential health-service coverage, yet many households still sell assets or incur debt when illness occurs. The ministry evaluates Sustainable Development Goal target 3.8 rather than service volume alone. Effective access and service quality are also being audited. Which two dimensions define progress toward universal health coverage?
options: Essential service coverage and financial risk protection
Hospital beds and medical tourism only
Life expectancy and birth rate only
Insurance enrollment without access or protection
explanation: Universal health coverage means that people receive needed quality services across promotion, prevention, treatment, rehabilitation and palliation without financial hardship. SDG 3.8 therefore tracks both service coverage and financial protection; enrollment alone does not prove effective access.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A donor-funded programme lowers one infection temporarily but draws staff away from maternal care and collapses when external financing ends. National planners want progress that strengthens multiple SDG health targets and survives funding transitions. Community participation and domestic financing are feasible. Which strategy is most appropriate?
options: Maintain a permanently isolated vertical project
Integrate disease priorities into resilient primary health-care and financing systems
Measure only short-term commodity distribution
Exclude communities from priority setting
explanation: Durable SDG progress depends on integrated primary health care, workforce, information, supply and financing systems that can deliver multiple essential services equitably. Targeted programmes may remain valuable, but they should strengthen rather than fragment national capacity and accountability.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A mobile health team screens schoolchildren for birth defects, nutritional deficiencies, childhood diseases and developmental delays. Children with suspected conditions are linked to referral and early-intervention services rather than merely given a health lecture. The children span the programme's eligible age groups. Which national child-health framework organizes these four categories?
options: Pradhan Mantri Surakshit Matritva Abhiyan
National Programme for Health Care of the Elderly
Rashtriya Bal Swasthya Karyakram
National Tuberculosis Elimination Programme
explanation: RBSK screens children for the four Ds: defects at birth, deficiencies, diseases and developmental delays including disability, with referral and early intervention. It spans specified child age groups and complements, rather than replaces, routine school and primary-care services.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A secondary school nominates two trained teachers to conduct participatory weekly sessions on nutrition, mental health, gender, substance-use prevention and healthy relationships. The teachers link students to services but do not diagnose or prescribe. Referral pathways to health facilities are active. Which role do they perform under the School Health and Wellness Programme?
options: Accredited Social Health Activists for every household
Medical officers conducting surgery
Laboratory surveillance officers
Health and Wellness Ambassadors
explanation: The School Health and Wellness Programme trains two teachers per school as Health and Wellness Ambassadors to deliver age-appropriate participatory health-promotion sessions and facilitate linkages. They are educators and connectors, not substitutes for clinical professionals or community ASHAs.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A cancer-screening programme detects disease two years earlier than usual clinical diagnosis. The date of death is unchanged because treatment has no effect, yet measured survival from diagnosis appears two years longer in the screened group. Stage distribution and cause of death are otherwise comparable. Which bias explains the apparent benefit?
options: Lead-time bias
Length bias
Recall bias
Berkson bias
explanation: Lead-time bias arises when earlier diagnosis advances the starting point used to measure survival without postponing death. Mortality rather than postdiagnosis survival is therefore the more reliable endpoint for screening benefit, ideally assessed in randomized or otherwise rigorous comparisons.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: Periodic screening disproportionately detects slowly progressive tumors because they remain in a detectable preclinical phase for longer. Aggressive tumors pass rapidly between scheduled rounds and present symptomatically between screens. Screen-detected cases therefore seem to have a better prognosis even without treatment benefit. Which bias is operating?
options: Lead-time bias only
Length bias
Interviewer bias
Confounding by indication
explanation: Length bias results from preferential detection of indolent disease with a long preclinical detectable phase, making screen-detected cases appear biologically favorable. Interval cancers often represent faster disease; mortality and overdiagnosis must be evaluated rather than relying on case survival alone.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A diagnostic pathway uses two independent tests and labels a person positive if either test is positive. The disease is serious and missing a case is considered more harmful than additional confirmatory referrals. Both component tests have imperfect sensitivity and specificity. Compared with either test alone, what is the usual effect of this parallel strategy?
options: Lower sensitivity and higher specificity
Higher sensitivity and higher specificity in every setting
Higher sensitivity and lower specificity
No change in false-positive results
explanation: Parallel testing classifies a person positive when any test is positive, so it captures more true cases and increases sensitivity but also creates more false positives and reduces specificity. Serial testing requires all specified positives and generally does the opposite.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A blood-bank algorithm first uses a highly sensitive screening assay and then requires a more specific supplemental assay before reporting a final positive result. The tests are applied in sequence, and indeterminate results follow a separate pathway. Disease prevalence is unchanged during testing. Which performance change is expected for the final serial rule?
options: Sensitivity increases while specificity decreases
Both predictive values become prevalence independent
Every false negative is eliminated
Specificity increases while sensitivity may decrease
explanation: Serial testing requires concordant sequential positivity, reducing false positives and increasing specificity, although some true cases may be lost and sensitivity can fall. Predictive values still depend on prevalence and on the conditional performance of the combined algorithm.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A national influenza programme selects representative hospitals and laboratories for detailed virologic and clinical reporting. The sites are chosen for quality and geographic coverage rather than to enumerate every case in the population. Data are used to track circulating strains and severity trends. What type of surveillance is this?
options: Sentinel surveillance
Universal case registration
One-time mass screening
Purely passive vital registration
explanation: Sentinel surveillance uses selected reporting sites to obtain high-quality, detailed and timely information on trends or pathogen characteristics. It is efficient but does not directly count every population case, so representativeness and site continuity must be considered.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: Routine clinicians submit disease reports without the surveillance team contacting facilities or searching records. Reporting is inexpensive and continuous, but completeness varies with clinician awareness and workload. During a suspected outbreak, the district considers actively calling facilities and reviewing registers. What describes the routine system?
options: Active surveillance
Passive surveillance
Sentinel laboratory surveillance only
Randomized surveillance
explanation: Passive surveillance relies on routine reporting initiated by healthcare providers and is economical but prone to underreporting and variable timeliness. Active surveillance deliberately seeks cases through calls, visits or record review and is often intensified during outbreaks or elimination efforts.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A healthcare facility treats microbiology laboratory waste before it leaves the laboratory area. The validated autoclave cycle reaches the specified temperature, pressure and holding time, but staff rely only on the machine display. The infection-control committee wants evidence that resistant organisms were actually inactivated. Which monitor provides the strongest routine biological verification?
options: Autoclave tape color alone
A thermometer reading without exposure time
A validated bacterial-spore indicator
Visual inspection of bag color
explanation: A biological indicator containing resistant bacterial spores directly challenges sterilization efficacy and is the most meaningful process verification when used at the required schedule. Chemical indicators and physical records are important for each cycle but do not alone prove microbial kill.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: An autoclave load contains tightly packed bags with little steam penetration. Chamber temperature reaches the set point, and external indicator tape changes color, yet internal biological testing fails. Staff propose increasing load density to save time. Which corrective action is most appropriate?
options: Ignore the failed biological indicator
Use external tape as proof of sterility
Seal bags completely against steam entry
Reduce load density and validate steam contact throughout the load
explanation: Successful moist-heat sterilization requires saturated steam contact at the correct temperature, pressure and time throughout the load. Overpacking and sealed air pockets prevent penetration; a failed biological indicator requires investigation, load recall when appropriate and cycle revalidation.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A flexible endoscope contacts mucous membranes but does not ordinarily enter sterile tissue. After thorough leak testing and manual cleaning, the device cannot tolerate steam sterilization. The unit needs the minimum Spaulding-level processing appropriate for safe reuse. Which level is required?
options: High-level disinfection
Low-level environmental cleaning only
No processing if a sheath was used
Incineration after every use
explanation: A flexible endoscope is a semicritical device and requires meticulous cleaning followed by high-level disinfection, rinsing, drying and storage according to validated protocols. Cleaning is indispensable because organic material impairs disinfectants; low-level treatment is inadequate for mucosal contact.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A reusable surgical instrument will enter sterile tissue during the next procedure. It is heat stable, visibly clean after decontamination and compatible with steam. Staff consider high-level disinfection sufficient because no spores were found in the previous case. Which processing endpoint is required?
options: Intermediate-level disinfection
Steam sterilization
Low-level disinfection
Dry surface wiping
explanation: Critical devices entering sterile tissue or the vascular system must be sterile, with steam sterilization preferred for compatible equipment. High-level disinfection is appropriate for semicritical items but may not reliably destroy all spores and therefore does not meet the critical-device requirement.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A national survey must ensure adequate representation of tribal, urban and rural populations, each of which is internally heterogeneous and differs greatly in size. Investigators can sample separately within each subgroup using known population lists and later apply weights. Precision for subgroup estimates is a major objective. Which sampling design is most appropriate?
options: Convenience sampling
Snowball sampling
Stratified random sampling
Simple random sampling without strata
explanation: Stratified random sampling divides the population into meaningful strata and samples within each, ensuring representation and often improving precision when strata differ. Weights can restore population totals when sampling fractions differ; convenience and snowball methods do not provide comparable probability inference.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A rapid coverage survey randomly selects villages, then surveys all eligible households within each selected village. Travel cost is reduced, but people within a village have correlated vaccination behavior. The analysis must account for this structure and unequal village size. Which design was used?
options: Systematic sampling of one national list
Stratified sampling with independent persons only
Purposive sampling
Cluster sampling
explanation: The first-stage selection of groups followed by sampling within selected groups is cluster sampling. It improves field efficiency but usually reduces statistical efficiency because observations within clusters correlate, requiring survey weights, cluster-aware variance estimation and design-effect adjustment.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A trial is designed to detect a clinically important mortality reduction. Investigators lower alpha from 0.05 to 0.01 while keeping effect size, variability and target power unchanged. Recruitment and follow-up methods are otherwise identical. What happens to the required sample size?
options: It increases
It decreases
It remains exactly unchanged
It becomes zero
explanation: A smaller alpha requires stronger evidence before rejecting the null, so more observations are needed to preserve power for the same effect and variance. Larger desired power, smaller effect size or greater variability also increase sample-size requirements.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A study concludes that a new screening test is not more sensitive than the standard, but the sample is small and the confidence interval includes a clinically important improvement. In truth, the new test really is better. The investigators failed to reject a false null hypothesis. Which statistical error occurred?
options: Type I error
Type II error
Selection bias
Information bias
explanation: A type II error is failure to reject a false null hypothesis, with probability beta; power is one minus beta. A type I error rejects a true null with probability alpha, while selection and information bias are systematic rather than random hypothesis-testing errors.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: Systolic blood pressure is measured in the same 60 adults before and three months after a salt-reduction intervention. The within-person differences are approximately normally distributed, and there is no separate control group. Investigators want to test whether the mean paired change differs from zero. Which test is appropriate?
options: Independent-samples t test
Chi-square test of independence
Paired t test
One-sample test of proportions
explanation: A paired t test analyzes the mean of within-person differences when those differences are approximately normal. An independent t test would ignore pairing and waste information, while chi-square and proportion tests apply to categorical rather than continuous paired change.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: Pain scores are recorded before and after treatment in the same small group. The paired differences are strongly skewed with several extreme values, and an ordinal interpretation is acceptable. A transformation does not yield a stable normal distribution. Which nonparametric test is most appropriate?
options: Mann-Whitney U test
Unpaired t test
Pearson chi-square test
Wilcoxon signed-rank test
explanation: The Wilcoxon signed-rank test evaluates paired or matched ordinal or continuous observations when the distribution of differences is not adequately normal, assuming a reasonably symmetric difference distribution for its usual location interpretation. Mann-Whitney is for independent groups.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A risk ratio is estimated as 0.78 with a 95% confidence interval from 0.61 to 0.99. The study is otherwise unbiased, the outcome is prespecified and the analysis is correctly performed. The null value for a ratio is 1.0. Which frequentist interpretation is appropriate?
options: The result excludes the null at the 5% two-sided level
There is a 95% probability the true value lies in this realized interval
The intervention benefits exactly 22% of every patient
The study proves no residual uncertainty
explanation: Because the two-sided 95% confidence interval excludes 1.0, the corresponding test rejects the null at alpha 0.05. In repeated sampling, 95% of intervals built by this method would cover the true value; the realized interval is not a posterior probability statement and uncertainty remains.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A large study reports a mean difference of 0.3 units with a 95% confidence interval from 0.2 to 0.4 and p below 0.001. Clinicians consider a difference of at least 5 units necessary to matter. Measurement bias is unlikely and the estimate is precise. Which conclusion is best?
options: Clinically important because p is small
Statistically significant but not clinically important
Not statistically significant
The null effect is proven true
explanation: A precise small effect can be highly statistically significant in a large sample while remaining far below the prespecified minimal clinically important difference. Interpretation should consider effect magnitude, confidence interval, harms, cost and patient priorities rather than p value alone.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A state plans rural facilities using conventional population norms for general plains areas. One proposed primary health centre would serve 30,000 people and link upward to a community health centre, while subcentres cover smaller catchments. Terrain is not classified as difficult or tribal. Which population norm is correct for this PHC?
options: About 5,000 people
About 120,000 people
About 30,000 people
About 500,000 people
explanation: The conventional rural norm in general plains areas is approximately one subcentre per 5,000 population, one PHC per 30,000 and one CHC per 120,000. Difficult, hilly or tribal areas use lower population thresholds because geographic access is harder.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A hilly tribal block has dispersed settlements and long travel times. Applying plains-area catchments would leave many villages without timely primary care, so planners use the difficult-area norms. Facility staffing and IPHS functions are considered separately. Which PHC population norm is traditionally applied in such areas?
options: About 3,000 people
About 80,000 people
About 200,000 people
About 20,000 people
explanation: The conventional difficult, hilly or tribal norm is roughly one PHC per 20,000 population, compared with 30,000 in plains areas. The corresponding traditional norms are about 3,000 for a subcentre and 80,000 for a CHC, subject to current state planning and IPHS considerations.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A newly diagnosed adult has microbiologically confirmed drug-susceptible pulmonary tuberculosis. Baseline liver and renal function permit standard therapy, pregnancy is excluded and there is no prior resistance. The programme uses daily fixed-dose combinations with weight-band dosing and adherence support. Which standard regimen structure is appropriate?
options: Two months HRZE followed by four months HRE
Six months isoniazid monotherapy
Two months HR followed by ten months streptomycin
One week rifampicin alone
explanation: Under NTEP, uncomplicated drug-susceptible TB is treated with a daily intensive phase of isoniazid, rifampicin, pyrazinamide and ethambutol followed by a continuation phase of isoniazid, rifampicin and ethambutol, with weight-band dosing and programme monitoring. Monotherapy promotes resistance.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A patient's molecular test detects rifampicin resistance before treatment begins. Additional molecular and phenotypic susceptibility testing is being arranged, and the patient is clinically stable. A clinician proposes adding one new drug to a failing first-line regimen while awaiting results. Which programme principle is correct?
options: Add a single active drug to a failing regimen
Construct an effective all-oral regimen using current resistance results and eligibility criteria
Continue ineffective first-line therapy indefinitely
Use injectable monotherapy for every resistant case
explanation: Drug-resistant TB requires an effective all-oral regimen selected from current national guidance, resistance pattern, prior exposure, age, pregnancy, comorbidity and drug interactions. Under the national DR-TB guideline published in 2025, BPaLM is the first-choice regimen for eligible people aged fourteen years or older with MDR/RR-TB; eligible alternatives include other shorter oral regimens, while ineligible patients need an individualized longer regimen. Adding one active drug to a failing regimen rapidly selects additional resistance.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: An adult receives a confirmed HIV diagnosis through the national testing algorithm. CD4 count is high, there is no opportunistic infection needing staged treatment and the patient is ready to begin therapy. Staff consider waiting until immunosuppression develops because the person feels well. Which public-health treatment policy applies?
options: Wait until CD4 falls below 200 cells per microliter
Use single-drug prophylaxis as lifelong treatment
Initiate antiretroviral therapy regardless of CD4 count
Defer therapy until an AIDS-defining illness occurs
explanation: Current HIV care follows test-and-treat: all people with confirmed HIV should be offered combination ART regardless of CD4 count, with readiness, interaction and opportunistic-infection assessment. Early suppressive treatment improves individual outcomes and prevents sexual transmission when suppression is sustained.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A person on ART reports perfect adherence, but routine viral-load testing shows persistent viremia. Treatment history, drug interactions and dispensing records are reviewed before changing the regimen. A single result could reflect adherence interruption or laboratory variation, and resistance may be emerging. Which programme step is most appropriate first?
options: Declare treatment failure from symptoms alone
Stop all ART without replacement
Add one antiretroviral to the same failing regimen
Provide enhanced adherence assessment and repeat viral load per protocol
explanation: A detectable or high viral load requires structured adherence and interaction assessment, targeted counselling and repeat testing at the recommended interval before confirming virologic failure, unless urgent clinical circumstances dictate otherwise. Confirmed failure then prompts an effective switch, never addition of a single drug.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A person has six anesthetic skin lesions and involvement of two peripheral nerves. Slit-skin smear is unavailable, and treatment must begin through the national leprosy programme without waiting for disability. The worker must classify disease for multidrug-therapy duration. Which operational category applies?
options: Multibacillary leprosy
Paucibacillary leprosy
Single-lesion indeterminate disease only
Noninfectious dermatitis requiring no follow-up
explanation: Under India's revised NLEP classification effective from 1 April 2025, multibacillary leprosy is defined by more than five skin lesions, involvement of one or more peripheral nerves, or bacilli on slit-skin smear; paucibacillary disease has one to five lesions, no nerve involvement and a negative smear. This patient meets both lesion and nerve criteria for multibacillary disease, and current programme MDT uses three drugs for both operational categories with category-appropriate duration.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A newly detected leprosy case has loss of protective sensation but no visible deformity or damage of the eyes, hands or feet. Visual acuity is preserved and ulceration is absent. The programme records disability grade at diagnosis to evaluate delay in detection. Which WHO disability grade is assigned?
options: Grade 0 disability
Grade 1 disability
Grade 2 disability
Disability cannot be graded without a skin smear
explanation: Grade 1 denotes loss of sensation without visible deformity or damage; grade 2 requires visible damage such as ulcer, clawing, foot drop, lagophthalmos or severe visual impairment. Recording grade 2 among new cases helps monitor late detection, but sensory loss already warrants protection and self-care.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A 4-year-old develops acute flaccid paralysis without trauma. Poliomyelitis is rare, but elimination requires sensitive surveillance for every compatible syndrome rather than waiting for clinical certainty. The child presents three days after paralysis onset, and stool collection can begin immediately. Which specimen strategy is considered adequate for AFP surveillance?
options: One throat swab after six months
One stool specimen after 60 days only
Two stool specimens 24-48 hours apart within 14 days of onset
Serum antibody testing without stool collection
explanation: Adequate AFP investigation obtains two stool specimens at least 24 hours apart and within 14 days of paralysis onset, with correct labeling and reverse cold chain to the laboratory. A 60-day examination assesses residual paralysis but does not replace timely virologic sampling.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A district reports no confirmed poliomyelitis for several years and proposes stopping acute flaccid-paralysis surveillance. Stool adequacy and non-polio AFP rates have recently fallen, suggesting missed cases rather than true absence. Environmental samples are collected in only one city. Which programme response is appropriate?
options: Stop reporting because polio is eliminated nationally
Investigate only vaccinated children
Use environmental surveillance as a complete replacement for AFP
Strengthen sensitive AFP surveillance and specimen quality
explanation: Polio-free status requires continued high-quality AFP surveillance, prompt investigation and adequate stool collection because importation or vaccine-derived virus can occur. Environmental surveillance is complementary and cannot replace nationwide clinical surveillance, particularly where performance indicators are declining.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A man with hazardous alcohol use is ambivalent about change. He understands medical risks but becomes defensive when lectured, and previous one-way warnings failed. The clinician wants a collaborative communication method that explores discrepancy and supports autonomy. Which approach is most appropriate?
options: Motivational interviewing with reflective listening
Authoritarian instruction without feedback
Fear messaging as the sole strategy
Avoiding discussion until dependence is severe
explanation: Motivational interviewing uses open questions, affirmations, reflective listening and summaries to elicit the person's own reasons for change while respecting autonomy. It is more patient-centered than one-way persuasion and can strengthen readiness without confrontation.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A community meeting on vaccine hesitancy begins with a long technical lecture. Participants cannot ask questions, rumors are not explored and the presenter receives no feedback about comprehension. Coverage remains unchanged despite good attendance. Which communication defect is most important?
options: Excessive use of interpersonal communication
Absence of two-way feedback
Too much audience participation
Overuse of teach-back
explanation: Effective health communication is two-way: feedback reveals understanding, concerns and barriers and permits message adaptation. Attendance or information transfer alone does not demonstrate communication success, while dialogue, local trust and teach-back can correct misinformation.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A nutrition programme tracks money spent, staff trained, children screened, anemia prevalence and child mortality. Managers want an immediate measure of delivered activity rather than resources or ultimate health change. They choose the number of children who actually received supplements. What type of indicator is this?
options: Input indicator
Impact indicator
Output indicator
Equity index only
explanation: Outputs are the direct products or services delivered, such as children supplemented or sessions completed. Inputs are resources, processes describe how activities occur, outcomes reflect intermediate health or behavior changes, and impact concerns long-term population health.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: Two screening strategies produce the same number of correctly detected cases, but one costs substantially less. Outcomes are measured in natural units rather than preference-weighted life years, and downstream treatment costs are included consistently. Incremental costs are calculated from the health-system perspective. Which economic evaluation compares cost per case correctly detected?
options: Cost-benefit analysis
Cost-minimization without proof of equal outcomes
Simple budget listing
Cost-effectiveness analysis
explanation: Cost-effectiveness analysis relates incremental costs to outcomes in natural units such as cases detected or deaths averted. Cost-benefit analysis monetizes both costs and benefits, while cost-minimization is justified only after outcomes are shown equivalent and a budget listing does not compare efficiency.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A district objective states only that maternal care should improve soon. It has no target population, baseline, measurable endpoint or deadline, so staff cannot assess accountability. Managers revise it using a recognized objective-setting framework. Which version is most suitable?
options: Increase first-trimester ANC registration from 62% to 80% by March 2027
Improve ANC as much as possible
Ensure better maternal health someday
Conduct many activities without a stated outcome
explanation: The revised objective is specific, measurable, achievable or assignable, relevant and time-bound, with a defined indicator, baseline, target and deadline. Vague intentions or activity counts cannot show whether the desired service outcome has been achieved.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A programme team lists reliable community health workers and strong political support, but also high staff turnover and an impending flood season. They classify internal and external factors before choosing a strategy. The classification is intended for strategic planning rather than impact estimation. Which planning tool organizes strengths, weaknesses, opportunities and threats?
options: Kaplan-Meier analysis
SWOT analysis
Bland-Altman analysis
Receiver-operating-characteristic analysis
explanation: SWOT analysis separates internal strengths and weaknesses from external opportunities and threats to support strategic planning. It structures judgment rather than proving causal impact, and the listed statistical plots answer survival, agreement or diagnostic-discrimination questions instead.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
topic: Natural history and transmission of disease
question: A food handler sheds Salmonella without symptoms after recovering from illness. The person can transmit infection, while the organism also persists in animal hosts. The outbreak team distinguishes the infected individual who harbors and disseminates the agent from the broader ecological habitat. Which term applies to the person?
options: Vector
Fomite
Convalescent carrier
Portal of entry
explanation: A convalescent carrier continues to harbor and shed an infectious agent after apparent clinical recovery. A reservoir is the usual habitat in which the agent lives or multiplies and may include humans, animals or environment, while a vector is a transmitting arthropod.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
topic: Natural history and transmission of disease
question: Tetanus spores persist in soil, but people with tetanus do not transmit the disease to contacts. A wounded patient acquires infection when spores are inoculated into devitalized tissue. The control strategy therefore emphasizes wound care and vaccination rather than isolation. Which epidemiologic description is correct?
options: Humans are the only reservoir and droplets are the route
Mosquitoes are the obligatory vector
Every case creates a propagated epidemic
Soil is the reservoir and transmission is not person to person
explanation: Clostridium tetani spores persist in soil and animal or human fecal contamination, entering through wounds; clinical cases do not spread tetanus between people. Prevention relies on active immunization, appropriate wound care and immunoglobulin when indicated, not contact isolation.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
topic: Natural history and transmission of disease
question: For a respiratory infection, an infected person becomes contagious two days before symptoms. The average time from infection to symptom onset is five days, and the average time from symptom onset in a primary case to symptom onset in a secondary case is four days. Exposure pairs are traced well enough to identify primary and secondary cases. Which interval is four days in this example?
options: Serial interval
Incubation period
Latent period
Generation time exactly by definition
explanation: The serial interval is the time between symptom onset in a primary case and symptom onset in a secondary case. Incubation runs from infection to symptoms, the latent period from infection to infectiousness, and generation time runs between infection events rather than symptom onsets.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
topic: Natural history and transmission of disease
question: A pathogen has a latent period shorter than its incubation period. Infected people can therefore transmit before recognizing symptoms, and symptom-based isolation alone misses part of the infectious period. Contact tracing resources are limited but available. Which implication follows?
options: Transmission begins only after symptoms
Presymptomatic contact management may be required
Incubation and latent periods must be identical
Case isolation after symptoms prevents every secondary case
explanation: When infectiousness begins before symptoms, presymptomatic transmission is possible and contact tracing, timely quarantine or prophylaxis, masking and other layered controls may be necessary. Symptom-based isolation remains useful but cannot intercept exposures that already occurred.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A district surveillance unit submits weekly data through the Integrated Health Information Platform. A health worker identifies a fever syndrome without a presumptive clinical diagnosis or laboratory confirmation. The reporting categories distinguish syndromic, presumptive and laboratory-confirmed cases. Under which category is this notification entered?
options: P, presumptive surveillance
L, laboratory-confirmed surveillance
S, syndromic surveillance
No report until a pathogen is cultured
explanation: IDSP uses S for syndromic reports based on symptom complexes, P for presumptive clinical diagnoses by medical officers and L for laboratory-confirmed disease. Early S reporting supports signal detection, while later clinical or laboratory information can refine classification.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A reporting unit sees no cases of a notifiable syndrome during the week. Staff plan to leave the electronic form blank, which would be indistinguishable from failure to report. The district requires complete denominator-aware surveillance and timely alerts. Which practice is correct?
options: Submit nothing when zero cases occur
Enter one artificial case to show activity
Wait for the annual report
Submit an explicit weekly NIL report
explanation: NIL reporting distinguishes a true zero from missing data and is essential for measuring reporting completeness and interpreting absence of signals. Fabricating a case is unacceptable, while delayed annual aggregation defeats the timeliness needed for outbreak detection and response.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A district evaluates iodine nutrition after reports of goiter and poor school performance. Household salt testing is useful for programme coverage, but the survey needs a population biomarker of recent iodine intake in school-age children. Individual values vary substantially from day to day, so the result will be interpreted at group level. Which indicator is preferred?
options: Median urinary iodine concentration
One child's serum thyroxine alone
Mean adult body-mass index
Household hemoglobin concentration
explanation: Median urinary iodine concentration in an appropriate representative group reflects recent population iodine intake because most absorbed iodine is excreted in urine. It is not a reliable individual diagnostic test from a single sample, so distribution and median are interpreted alongside iodized-salt coverage and thyroid indicators.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A salt iodization programme has high factory compliance, but retail samples in humid coastal markets often lose iodine before household use. Packaging is porous, bags are stored open near heat and consumers add salt early during prolonged cooking. The programme wants to preserve iodine from production to consumption. Which intervention is most appropriate?
options: Store salt uncovered beside the stove
Use moisture-proof storage and add iodized salt late
Wash salt repeatedly before use
Replace iodization with universal thyroxine tablets
explanation: Iodine is lost through moisture, heat and prolonged storage, so sealed moisture-resistant packaging, cool dry storage and adding salt toward the end of cooking help preserve it. Quality assurance at production and retail remains essential; routine thyroxine cannot replace population salt iodization.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A healthy 4-month-old is growing appropriately on breast milk. The family plans to add water during hot weather because the infant appears thirsty, but there is no medical indication for supplementation. Safe-water access is uncertain and early foods may displace breast milk. Which public-health feeding recommendation applies?
options: Introduce diluted animal milk at four months
Give water after every feed in hot weather
Continue exclusive breastfeeding without water until six months
Stop breastfeeding when complementary food starts
explanation: Exclusive breastfeeding means breast milk without other food or drink, including water, except medicines or prescribed supplements, for the first six months. Breast milk supplies needed water even in hot climates and avoids contamination and displacement risks from unnecessary feeds.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A healthy infant completes six months of age and has good head control. Breastfeeding is established, but growth velocity has begun to slow and breast milk alone no longer meets all nutrient requirements. The family can prepare thick, diverse foods hygienically and continue responsive feeding. Which recommendation is appropriate?
options: Continue only breast milk until twelve months
Replace breast milk completely with thin cereal water
Delay all animal-source food until school age
Start age-appropriate complementary feeding while continuing breastfeeding
explanation: Complementary foods should begin at about six months with adequate frequency, energy density, diversity and responsive feeding while breastfeeding continues to two years or beyond. Thin cereal water is nutrient poor, and delaying complementary feeding risks growth faltering and micronutrient deficiency.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A pregnant woman with obstructed labor reaches a public facility without money or blood donors. Emergency cesarean delivery, medicines, diagnostics, blood and referral transport are required. Staff suggest that only the operation is free while the family must buy consumables outside. Which national entitlement directly contradicts that demand?
options: Janani Shishu Suraksha Karyakram
Pradhan Mantri Surakshit Matritva Abhiyan only
National Tobacco Control Programme
Integrated Disease Surveillance Programme
explanation: JSSK provides pregnant women using public facilities with free and cashless delivery including cesarean section, drugs, diagnostics, blood, diet and transport entitlements. It is intended to remove out-of-pocket barriers; PMSMA is a fixed-day antenatal service rather than the full delivery entitlement package.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A 3-month-old infant develops sepsis and needs admission, diagnostics, antibiotics, blood products and transfer between public facilities. The family is told that free newborn entitlements ended at 28 days. Current programme provisions extend specified services beyond the neonatal period. Which statement is correct?
options: Entitlements end immediately after birth
JSSK entitlements extend to sick infants up to one year
Only outpatient registration is free
Transport is excluded for every sick infant
explanation: JSSK was expanded to cover sick newborns and infants up to one year in public facilities, including free drugs, diagnostics, blood where required and transport entitlements. Local implementation must be monitored, but families should not be charged merely because the child is older than 28 days.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A woman in labor is refused admission at two public facilities because she lacks documents and is considered difficult to manage. She is shouted at during examination and asked to purchase routine supplies despite an emergency. The district wants a programme explicitly centered on assured dignified, respectful care without denial. Which initiative is most relevant?
options: LaQshya only
Mission Indradhanush
SUMAN
NTEP
explanation: Surakshit Matritva Aashwasan, or SUMAN, emphasizes assured, dignified, respectful, quality maternal and newborn care at no cost with zero denial. LaQshya improves labor-room and maternity-OT quality but does not replace the broader entitlement and respectful-care commitments.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A district hospital has high institutional delivery coverage but inconsistent triage, infection prevention, respectful care and emergency readiness in the labor room and maternity operating theatre. The problem is facility-process quality rather than failure to attend antenatal clinic. Maternal deaths are reviewed, but most identified gaps occur after admission. Which national quality-improvement initiative directly targets these areas?
options: Pradhan Mantri Surakshit Matritva Abhiyan
School Health and Wellness Programme
National Leprosy Eradication Programme
LaQshya labor-room quality initiative
explanation: LaQshya focuses on improving quality of care in labor rooms and maternity operation theatres, including clinical processes, respectful maternity care and complication readiness. PMSMA targets comprehensive fixed-day antenatal assessment and therefore does not directly solve intrapartum facility-process failures.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A 30-year-old dies from postpartum hemorrhage twelve days after delivery. The death is directly related to pregnancy, not accidental or incidental, and registration is complete. The district reports maternal mortality using live births as the denominator. Into which numerator should this death be counted?
options: Maternal deaths occurring during pregnancy or within 42 days of termination
All deaths among women aged 15-49 regardless of cause
Only deaths occurring inside the labor room
Infant deaths within 28 days
explanation: A maternal death is the death of a woman while pregnant or within 42 days of termination, from a cause related to or aggravated by pregnancy or its management, excluding accidental or incidental causes. Postpartum hemorrhage at twelve days therefore belongs in the maternal-death numerator.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A state records 120 qualifying maternal deaths and 400,000 live births in one year. Ascertainment of late maternal deaths beyond 42 days is analyzed separately, and accidental deaths are excluded. The programme wants the conventional maternal mortality ratio. What is the reported value?
options: 3 per 1,000 pregnancies
30 maternal deaths per 100,000 live births
30 per 1,000 women of reproductive age
300 per 100,000 total population
explanation: Maternal mortality ratio is maternal deaths divided by live births multiplied by 100,000: 120/400,000 x 100,000 = 30. It is a ratio of obstetric risk per live birth, not a rate based on woman-years or total population.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A term newborn is discharged after an uncomplicated institutional birth. Feeding is established, but the family lives remotely and early jaundice, sepsis or weight loss could be missed. An ASHA is assigned for scheduled postnatal home visits and referral of danger signs. Which strategy is being implemented?
options: Home-Based Care for Young Child only after infancy
Pradhan Mantri Surakshit Matritva Abhiyan
Home-Based Newborn Care
National AIDS Control Programme
explanation: Home-Based Newborn Care provides scheduled ASHA home visits during the neonatal period to support breastfeeding, thermal care, cord care, danger-sign recognition, low-birth-weight follow-up and timely referral. It complements facility care and does not replace clinical evaluation when illness is suspected.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A state reviews progress toward the national goal of reducing newborn deaths and stillbirths. Facility-based newborn care, home visits, kangaroo mother care and quality improvement are coordinated under one strategic framework. The target is to reach single-digit neonatal mortality and stillbirth rates by 2030. Which framework states this goal?
options: National AIDS Control Programme
National Strategic Plan for Malaria only
Integrated Vector Management
India Newborn Action Plan
explanation: The India Newborn Action Plan aligns newborn and stillbirth reduction strategies toward single-digit neonatal mortality and stillbirth rates by 2030. It integrates care across preconception, antenatal, intrapartum, facility and community settings rather than relying on one isolated intervention.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: At a banquet, 80 people ate custard and 40 became ill, while 120 did not eat custard and 12 became ill. Case definition and follow-up are identical for both groups, and nearly all guests were interviewed. Investigators want the association between custard consumption and acute gastroenteritis. What is the risk ratio?
explanation: The attack rate is 40/80=50% among exposed and 12/120=10% among unexposed, so the risk ratio is 0.50/0.10=5.0. This strong association supports the custard hypothesis but must be integrated with timing, dose-response, kitchen investigation and laboratory evidence.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: During the same outbreak, another dish was eaten by nearly every guest, leaving only three unexposed people. Its exposed attack rate is high, but the comparison estimate has an extremely wide confidence interval. The team considers declaring that dish causal solely because its crude attack rate is largest. Which interpretation is best?
options: A high exposed attack rate alone proves causation
The sparse unexposed group makes the association imprecise
Confidence intervals are irrelevant in outbreaks
Universal exposure guarantees no confounding
explanation: When very few people are unexposed, the reference attack rate and risk ratio are unstable, producing imprecise estimates. Investigators should compare exposed and unexposed groups, examine confidence intervals and consistency, and integrate the epidemic curve and environmental evidence rather than rank exposed rates alone.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: An unvaccinated adult is bitten deeply on the hand by an untraceable stray dog. The wound bleeds, no nerve tissue vaccine was used previously and treatment begins immediately. The wound is washed thoroughly with soap and running water, and a modern rabies vaccine schedule is available. What additional biological is indicated?
options: Tetanus immunoglobulin as the only rabies prophylaxis
Antibiotics without rabies vaccination
Rabies immunoglobulin infiltrated into and around the wounds
Observation alone for an unavailable animal
explanation: A transdermal bleeding bite is a category III exposure requiring immediate wound care, rabies vaccine and rabies immunoglobulin for a previously unvaccinated person. As much immunoglobulin as anatomically feasible is infiltrated into and around all wounds without delaying vaccine.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A person who completed a documented modern pre-exposure rabies series is licked on abraded skin by a suspect animal. Wound cleansing is performed and public-health risk assessment confirms a true exposure. The patient is immunocompetent and has no prior use of obsolete vaccines. Which post-exposure regimen is appropriate?
options: Full vaccine series plus immunoglobulin
Rabies immunoglobulin alone
No wound care or vaccine under any circumstance
Booster vaccine doses without rabies immunoglobulin
explanation: Previously vaccinated people receive prompt wound care and the recommended booster vaccine schedule, but rabies immunoglobulin is not given because immune memory is already established. A documented valid prior series must be confirmed, and exposure management should not await symptoms.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A district approaching malaria elimination detects one locally acquired case after several months without transmission. The case is laboratory confirmed, travel history is carefully reviewed and vector receptivity remains high. Officials argue that one case is too few to justify field investigation. Which surveillance response is appropriate in an elimination setting?
options: Investigate the case promptly and classify its origin and focus
Wait for a large seasonal outbreak
Count only inpatient malaria deaths
Stop vector surveillance after one negative month
explanation: In elimination settings, every confirmed case is an actionable surveillance event requiring prompt notification, epidemiologic classification, focus investigation and targeted response. Low incidence increases rather than decreases the importance of finding imported, introduced, indigenous or relapsing transmission.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: India's current malaria strategy uses phased interruption of indigenous transmission followed by prevention of re-establishment. A programme presentation distinguishes the nearer operational milestone from the longer elimination goal. Managers are aligning district plans with the National Strategic Plan 2023-27. Which pairing is correct?
options: Elimination by 2025 with no later prevention phase
Zero indigenous cases targeted by 2027, with malaria elimination by 2030
Zero indigenous cases only after 2040
No time-bound national elimination goal
explanation: The National Strategic Plan 2023-27 aims toward interruption of indigenous transmission by 2027 as a key operational milestone, supporting the national goal of malaria elimination by 2030. Surveillance and prevention of re-establishment remain essential after local transmission is interrupted.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: An adult has cough, weight loss and a chest radiograph suggestive of pulmonary tuberculosis. The patient has a prior treatment history, so rifampicin resistance must be assessed rapidly rather than inferred from a smear. Sputum can be collected and a WHO-endorsed molecular platform is available. Which initial test best serves the programme objective?
options: Tuberculin skin test alone
Chest radiograph alone
Rapid molecular test detecting tuberculosis and rifampicin resistance
Routine bacterial culture without mycobacterial testing
explanation: A rapid molecular diagnostic such as a nucleic-acid amplification test can confirm M. tuberculosis and detect rifampicin resistance early, enabling appropriate treatment and further resistance testing. Smear and radiography contribute but cannot establish rifampicin susceptibility.

subject: Community Medicine (PSM)
question: A household contact of infectious pulmonary tuberculosis is asymptomatic and has no evidence of active disease after a proper symptom and diagnostic evaluation. HIV status and age are considered, and drug interactions are reviewed. The programme aims to prevent progression from infection to disease. Which intervention should be considered according to eligibility?
options: Standard multidrug treatment for active TB without diagnosis
BCG revaccination as sole contact management
No follow-up because symptoms are absent
Tuberculosis preventive treatment
explanation: Eligible contacts in whom active TB has been excluded should receive an approved tuberculosis preventive-treatment regimen with adherence and toxicity support. Contact investigation also includes symptom surveillance and infection-control counselling; absence of symptoms at one visit does not eliminate future risk.
